Sentence: You wake before the harbor fully remembers itself.
The Tide Lab rocks with a patient, familiar rhythm that matches the motion of your chest.
You sit on a stool that has been sanded smooth by a

dozen hands, roll the edge of your leather-bound notebook back against your palm, and wind your brass dive watch the way your mother once wound a sewing machine—slow, deliberate, as if the watch could turn tides
by insistence alone.
The strip of silver at your temple glints when you tuck a curl behind your ear.
You used to think that streak was a fault of the sea—salt taken from an ocean that took your childhood
you have been where water and memory meet, and you are still standing.
scalloped marsh edges, stiching lines where cordgrass might root, notes on sediment accretion.
The Tide Lab’s task lamps throw warm cones on
stacked maps.
Outside, the city is a smear of neon and rain—promenade lights blurred in puddles, the distant silhouette of the PolySilo rising like a thumb against the low sky.
salinity gradients for the Drowned District pilot, tidal amplitude over three months, plant survival rates by cohort.
The instruments hum.
A green readout blinks in a steady, encouraging rhythm.
You are here to prove something that feels, right now, almost like a stubborn hymn: nature can be engineered to heal itself if people get the chance to steward it.
Not because you hate engineering—because you trust the tide more than the ledger.
practical warmth, a little clipped, like a photograph developed too fast but still clear.
His amber-brown eyes flash in your memory—the
soft laugh lines at their corners, the sun-bleached tips of hair that always look like they’ve been brushed by wind.
You imagine him somewhere between the waterline and a drafting table, camera hanging at his neck,
boots muddy.
You smile despite the salt on your lips.
a photo of a modular berm detail; hand-drawn notes in the margin; a question.
You trace the diagram with your finger as if the ink might teach you rhythm.
The berm looks like a small promise—woven coir rolls locked by plantings, a removable core that lets the marsh breathe.
It doesn’t scream corporate scale.
It whispers possible.
• Trace the berm's curve on paper
You press your pen harder and redraw the line until the curve feels like a shore under your fingertips.
The motion steadies you—data begins to assemble itself into a plan.
• Reply immediately to Jonah Reyes with technical questions
You draft a quick volley of questions—soil compaction numbers, load-bearing tolerances—your fingers flying.
The engineer in you wakes, measured and precise.

You fold the message back into the page and stand.
The deck smells of iron and kelp; your boots make a soft scuff against wet steel.
Professor Asha Rao is already at the main bench, spectacles
fogged at the edges and a stack of archived tidal charts fanned beside her.
Her braid gleams like a silver rope.
She looks up when you arrive, and the room softens.
You're early, Mara Ellis.' (She smiles.
The name sounds different from how your mother used it—less intimate, more steady.) 'You take that as a good sign.
You set the berm sketch beside the others and feel the buoyant thrum of possibility.
Professor Asha leans in, fingers brushing a patch of salt.
She points at a map.
The pilot's numbers are holding.
Not miraculous, but consistent.
Sediment accretion is trending up and the cordgrass cohorts are showing higher than expected survival.
Higher than expected by how much?
Three percent above projection in the inner channels, five percent where we added the tidal breaks last month.
It's the kind of blip that becomes a proof point when you can replicate it.

You bite your tongue, wanting to add every caveat—you've already learned how quickly politicians love a single good number and how easily corporations co-opt a narrative—but the hope blooms anyway, warm and cautious.
Maya tumbles aboard like a sunburst.
She smells of espresso and street citrus.
She drops a thermos at your feet and slaps a hand on your shoulder—half-sister shove, full-sister affection.
You look like you haven't slept.
Or you've been up writing love letters to marsh grass.
They're technical notes, thank you very much.
Right—technical love letters.
Save me a line when you win the council over.
You grin.
She sits beside you and pulls a battered radio toward her.
Her eyes scan the reports on the tablet; her hands are steady where hers used to make drinks.
She has the neighborhood in her chest—every meeting, every flyer, every scraped knee.
Old Mate stopped by last night.
He said the reeds look like they're breathing again.
Said it slowly, like he's not used to saying anything optimistic.
Mateo's weathered voice, a neighbor catching a seedling.
The pilot in the Drowned District is not a headline yet, but it's breathing, and that counts.
Did he bring any more stories?
Any new cautions?
Always cautions.
He kept saying, 'A little structure to hold them while they learn to stand.' He liked the look of the trial plots.
That's the whole point—we're not building walls, Maya.
We're teaching the shore to stand.
Then teach it well,' she says, but there's warmth under the challenge.
'Also—Professor Rao wanted to talk after the meeting.
I wanted to ask if you would present the pilot's visuals at the faculty session next week.
Not the council—yet.
But people who sit in the right rooms.
We need a better frame before we step into chambers.
You feel the familiar prickle of nerves and possibility.
You imagine a room where your data slides under the eyes of people who can multiply your pilot into policy.
The idea tastes like copper and ocean spray.
I can do that.
I can show the replication plan, the cost-benefit that favors living shorelines if you include social resilience metrics.
Good.
I'll link you with Councilor Patel's aide—political threads and scientific threads together.
It improves our odds.
You nod.
Each small connection is a stitch.
The Tide Lab feels more than a lab; it's a loom.
You motor toward the Drowned District with two volunteers and a crate of plantings.
The tide sits low enough to let you trudge through mud without drowning in it—enough to show the difference between survival and
woven coir rolls, newly planted spartina, tiny stakes marking study sites.
The marsh edge that was thinning a season ago now shows scallops where sediment is catching.
Sediment traps you set earlier are fuller; the soil smells richer, less like rot and more like possibility.
Kids from
the neighborhood are poking a small wooden boat into the shallows; an old man—Old Mate—knocks his knuckles against a piling and grins in a way that pulls at your ribs.
having something taken doesn't mean you stop making.
This is it.
Look at this root structure—it's binding faster than our models expected.
If we scale, we can buffer more neighborhoods than we lost.
Volunteer Scaling will mean more materials, more volunteers.
And more eyes—politicians won't look away once they see it working.
Then we make them look at the right things: the people living here, the savings in displacement costs, the biodiversity gains.
• Take a sample for lab analysis
You scoop a small core and tuck it into your bag with meticulous care.
You need numbers to turn hope into policy.
• Tell a nearby child how cordgrass works
You crouch and explain how the roots knot the mud together.
Her eyes get wide; she presses her tiny hand to the soil, triumphant.
The moment recalibrates what 'proof' means to you.

'For the people who give us back our edges.' It’s modest and
enormous at the same time.
You tuck the jar into your bag with the sediment sample and the berm sketch.
Each is a token of faith.
Back on the Tide Lab, Jonah Reyes waits at the rail, slate-gray jacket flaking with salt, camera slung and palms still dusty.
He looks like someone who belongs in both the lab and the dock—an engineer
who remembers the name of the tide more than the name of a spreadsheet.
Up close, the warmth in his eyes is a kind of honest light.
Hey.
You look like you spent the morning convincing the coast to forgive you.
You laugh.
He hands over a rolled detail drawing—a modular berm with interchangeable cores and planting slots.
This is promising.
The modularity could let maintenance be community-led, too.
That's the idea.
Keep the tech small enough to fix with hand tools and civic will.
Speaking of—Professor Asha said your numbers are looking good.
They are.
Enough to ask for more attention.
Good.
Because I was talking to a contractor who might redirect some surplus materials if Council gives the nod.
Small wins become supply chains if we push hard.
You pause.
The mention of contractors could mean anything—old alliances, new compromises.
You are wary of quick fixes wrapped in helpful language.
But Jonah Reyes’s voice has an earnestness that is hard to mistrust.
If it stays community-run—if we keep oversight—then it's useful.
If it becomes a way to displace maintenance costs onto neighbors, then it becomes another wall.
Agreed.
I want solutions that keep families here, not move them somewhere else 'safer' so someone else can build on the waterfront.
He looks at you, and in that look there is a bridge being built out of ordinary attention.
The conversation unfolds with a dozen small turns—questions about anchoring methods, debates over maintenance schedules, a sidetrack about
you worry he sometimes softens his stance to get approvals; he worries you might be uncompromising to the point of losing momentum.
The exchanges
are not storms; they are negotiations, tides drawing in and out.
Can I show you a detail?
It's small, but it might be what lets the city sign off without wrecking the ecology.
Show me.
He spreads out a sketch.
His hands are sure; the diagram is functional and humane, a compromise that doesn't betray your core.
You run your finger along the line he designed and the curve fits into your own sketch like a missing puzzle piece.
• Ask Jonah Reyes about his compromises
You push—politely, sharply.
He hesitates, then offers a candid line about balancing permits and people.
You taste both relief and caution.
• Focus on the technical advantages
You talk details—porosity, vegetative establishment timeframes, community maintenance schedules.
The conversation becomes kinetic and hopeful, two minds drafting a shared scaffold.
Continue
Jonah Reyes lingers as the afternoon folds into an amber dusk.
You stand at the rail and watch the Drowned District shrink into a silhouette, the new plots visible as dark, patient stitches on the water.
Your chest feels open and heavy in equal measure—hope tied to responsibility.
Professor Asha approaches with a thermal mug and a look that says she has been moving a chessboard without telling you all the plays.
Council isn't a single hurdle, Mara Ellis.
It's a hundred small rooms.
You'll present to faculty; if that goes well, I'll arrange a briefing.
And I've emailed Councilor Patel.
Your pilot is beginning to look like a policy lever.
What should I emphasize?
Emphasize replicability, community benefit, and cost curves that show long-term savings when you include social metrics.
Bring people—voices from the Drowned District.
Data alone doesn't move faces in a room.
I will.
I'll bring Old Mate if he'll come.
(softly) 'Bring your conviction.
But remember—conviction is tempered by strategy.
You have both.'
pilot to program, sketches to scale.
The day has given you something rare—a minor success that does not feel pyrrhic.
It feels like momentum.
Jonah Reyes stands a step away, hands tucked into his jacket pockets, watching you plot.
You can see the way he measures the world—technically, but with room for tenderness.
You both know the other major player
Elias Crowe, whose sleek solutions sit on brochures and lobby tables and who favors decisive, centralized fixes.
You have not met him in person yet, but his shadow is a ledger in the
margins of every permit and proposal you study.
You touch the brass of your watch, feeling the little ridges where it has been worn thin.
It is not counting time for you so much as choices—each wind a small insistence that you will take this next step deliberately.
Tomorrow we finalize what you'll show.
Tonight, rest.
Momentum likes sleep.
Tomorrow we take it a room at a time.
You pack your notebook, the berm detail, the sediment sample, and the jar of clams.
Jonah Reyes offers to walk you to the ladder.
For a moment, the harbor is quiet in a way that feels like blessing rather than absence.
Thanks for today.
(smiling) 'Bring your stubbornness.
It makes good policy.'
You both laugh—an honest sound that pockets itself in the ferry's hull.
You stand at the rail a moment longer, letting the harbor wash the day into a slow glow.
The pilot's numbers sit in your notebook like small truths.
The community is stirring with cautious pride.
Jonah
Reyes's presence is a tangible possibility.
Professor Asha is already threading political routes.
The next step—the council corridors, the wider rooms where decisions multiply—feels close enough to taste.
You feel the tide of the chapter turn in your favor.
Hope is not naive; it's earned, measured, and ready to be argued for.
You fold the berm sketch into your pocket, tighten the strap on your boots, and name the next day's work like a vow—present, explain, connect.
The city's glass heart might be sterile and sharp, but you intend to warm it, one community-proof at a time.
You arrive with the salt of the harbor still in the cuffs of your jacket, the brass of your watch warm under your palm.
It is the same watch you wore on the ferry last night
messy,
necessary, real.
curled reeds bound with twine, strips of recycled oyster-shell mesh, tiny pilings artfully staggered in damp clay.
Jonah kneels at the edge of the display, arranging
laminated photos with a practiced hand.
When he hands you a print — a wide-angle shot of the experimental berm at low tide — the woven fishing-line ring on his right thumb flashes like a tiny,
deliberate lighthouse.
I managed a golden hour shot before the crew left.
Thought you might like the perspective for the board.
You take the print; his fingers brush yours for a beat longer than necessary.
You feel your chest loosen in a way your single-minded notes and sediment graphs never managed.
Practicality and warmth in the same
person; it makes you stubborn in a new way — stubborn to protect this small constellation of things you love.
Maya is already there, leaning against the railing with her neon-yellow coat splotched with damp mud from the community plots.
Her impatience is audible in the way she taps her boot on the wood.
They're late.
They're always late, and then they want everything perfect.
People are cold and wet, Mara.
We can't be neat about this.
We can't be messy about the demonstration either.
Precision convinces permits.
Stories keep us human.
We need both.
Maya scoffs but nods.
She jabs a finger toward a small cluster of elderly residents listening to Old Mate.
Old Mate — Mateo Alvarez — speaks with the cadence of tide charts read aloud.
His skin smells faintly of tar and old rope; his voice collects attention like gulls collect scraps.
Back when the harbor remembered boats and not barricades, the seawall let the storm pass.
The rats under the decking saved themselves then, and we— we learned which moorings to trust.
These ideas of yours…' (he pats a reed bundle) 'are not new to these eyes.
But they take people believing.
People, not numbers.
A reporter threads through the crowd, camera balanced on one shoulder.
Her mic has the municipal station logo; the lens drinks in the damp light.
She asks a question you expected and have rehearsed for a different kind of room.
Reporter How does a living breakwater respond to a surge compared to an engineered wall?
Aren't you risking houses for—
Professor Asha slips in beside you then, her braid unspooling just enough that salt flecks the silver.
She sets down a slim folder of archival charts — old bathymetries, annotated maps showing marsh line retreat over decades.
Her presence is a small, steadying authority.
It's not an either/or.
Living structures dissipate energy differently; the data show sediment trapping increases over time when combined with managed retreat corridors.
We have the long-term records.
Reporter So no immediate guarantees?
Never guarantees.
But measured resilience that keeps people in place while ecosystems adapt has a track record.
Councilor Anika arrives with an umbrella that refuses to stop dripping.
Her expression is courteous, almost surgical; she watches without applause, a habit of seeing both headlines and spreadsheets.
Ms.
Ellis, these are compelling models.
My concern is scale: what you show here is persuasive for a neighborhood—but can it convince the funding committees and the contractors who build at scale?
You meet her gaze.

She is the hinge between your work and municipal approval; you know she has to balance everything you've already said with the city's appetite for decisive measures — measures Elias Crowe sells expertly.
We're proposing a hybrid implementation.
Start neighborhood-scale: living breakwaters, marsh terraces that buy time and sediment.
Use those results to inform scalable designs—less destructive than a monolithic seawall, more adaptive than temporary pumps.
And the timeline?
Pilot season within six months.
Monitoring through community stewards.
If the trial demonstrates predicted accretion and reduced overtopping, we can scale adaptively.
Jonah Reyes adds, hands moving, explaining with the quiet mechanics of an engineer who likes to show instead of assert.
We've modeled wave attenuation across three storm scenarios.
With the reef matrix in place and the marsh elevation response we simulate, peak velocities drop enough to prevent overtopping for the 10-year event and reduce damage in the 25-year event.
A murmur of impressed surprise runs through the crowd.
Maya grins and mutters something you can't quite hear; you feel the momentum shift — not a tidal wave, but a steady, insistent swell.
• Stand by Jonah and let him finish the technical run-through
You step closer to Jonah, letting him take the technical lead.
His voice strengthens; you watch the way the audience relaxes around his details, and a small, private surge of pride rises in you.
• Take the mic and steer the message toward community stewardship
You fold the technical into a story — the baker down the block who will tend marsh plots, the kids who will track birds.
Faces soften; some nod, and Maya mouths 'good' like a benediction.
The demonstration continues with Old Mate's interruption and the community's response.
Old Mate interrupts, not unkindly, when Jonah mentions monitoring.
Monitoring?
Then who's going out when the rain comes?
Who will be the eye?
You talk 'bout data like a ledger, child.
We talk 'bout who wakes when the storm bell rings.
You promise to include both, yes?
You are the ledger.
The people are the monitors.
The data is for the councils to trust you, Old Mate.
We want the city to give you tools, not replace you.
Old Mate's jaw moves; for a moment his face is unreadable, then it unfurls into a slow nod.
The crowd shifts; a woman near the railing wipes a tear into a folded handkerchief and claps softly.

Professor Asha steps forward with archival overlays projected briefly from a tablet onto a damp piece of plywood; the map glows faintly, halos of data tracing the city's erosion like constellation lines.
She points to an area where your berm pilot produced sediment gains.
These layers correspond.
Your field results aren't anomalies — they follow historical accretion pathways we've documented.
This matters for council votes; they respond to precedent and to risk avoidance numbers.
she notices.

Maya squeals under her breath and then shushes it, trying to be dignified in the public moment.
you are elated, but you also feel the old, familiar hard line
don't trade long-term integrity for short-term cards.

A local blogger angles for a soundbite; someone streams the demonstration; a neighbor sweeps up mud and hands you a handful of broken shells as an offering for the marsh plots.
an anonymous tip, rudimentary and ominous — NovaSeis has been asking questions about the Drowned District and the council schedule.

a pressure building behind the throat.

Her expression flickers—politician recalibrating—and she asks for the source.
The mood becomes cautiously strategic; the room leans toward procedure.

You tuck the warning away.
For now the crowd's faith and the signed commitments feel like armor.
You choose to build momentum, believing exposure will come at the hearing.
The assembly continues; planning and commitments proceed as the council hearing is scheduled.
We are not here to romanticize loss or to postpone hard decisions.
We're here to propose that we can keep people in place while strengthening our shores.
If we win a pilot, we will have evidence.
If we fail, we will still have learned something to keep our neighbors safe.
A woman in the crowd — a teacher from the block school — raises her hand.
Teacher Will there be jobs?
People need work to stay.
Not just promises of safety.
Maintenance crews, monitoring teams, nurseries for marsh plants — these are jobs you can train for locally.
We'll include training in the pilot package.
We want local people running these projects.
You watch Jonah speak, the crowd reacting; the teacher's face brightens.
Your heart, stubborn as it is, opens a sliver wider.
There's romance in shared purpose — the small, dangerous hope that you and Jonah could be partners in both policy and life.
I'll provide the archival package for the council.
Historical data plus your recent pilot will make the case stronger than either alone.
Evidence and community testimony together—it's persuasive.
Councilor Anika's attention narrows to procedural focus.
Schedule me the hearing.
Bring me a short packet — models, the monitoring plan, public commitments.
I'll request an early slot.
Expect a formal calendar notice.
Applause; careful, constructive applause.
You feel the wood under your boots as though it were a stage you have already walked across.
Momentum, at last, has the shape of policy.
your pilot sits on the same map as a corporation that moves fast and loud.
Their proposals are rumored to promise immediate certainty, at the cost of local nuance.
Maya's face hardens.
Old Mate looks out toward the water, his features pulled into a line you cannot read easily.
You feel the decision circle closing, but not sealing.
The city has offered you a hearing; the council has a date.
The next step — how to argue in that room, who will carry which message,
visible only in outline, but crucial to navigate toward.
You tuck the print Jonah gave you into your notebook.
The paper is slightly damp, curling at the edges where the sea-spray hit.
You press your thumb to the image, feeling the faint grain of the
salty paper, and name the choice you'll have to make soon, even before the hearing begins.
A hearing date and a crowd's tentative trust.
A corporate shadow at the edges.
The Promenade hums with the small, electric possibility that public attention brings.
You can either step into a partnership that
may compromise some ideals for scale, take NovaSeis on directly, or pursue the slow policy-first route that waits for proof to change minds.
All three paths feel both dangerous and necessary.
You step into the light-washed amphitheater with your notebook pressed to your chest.
The chamber smells of rain-damp wool and disinfectant; the municipal coffee has a bitter, familiar comfort.
The floor beneath your boots is cool and echoes each small decision as a click.

Councilor Anika at her panel, expressions from local organizers to corporate reps arranged in neat rows, and Jonah Reyes two seats down, his jacket rolled at the sleeves, bandana bunched in his pocket like a talisman.
Professor Asha stands behind you, steady as a lighthouse.
You know the models.
Breathe.
She squeezes your shoulder once, no fuss.
You nod.
Your cuffs—coral-stained from long days measuring berm cores—feel like a confession under the sterile lights.
You smooth your jacket, although the salt stains won't come out.
The holographic coastline shivers, and with a touch to the console the map blooms where your data will appear.
Councilor Patel, members of the council, neighbors—our community-based pilot is modeled on three years of tide data, sediment capture, and living-root retention studies.
This map—' (you gesture; the berms glow) '—shows how distributed, nature-based interventions reduce frontal erosion and retain nearshore habitat, while lowering immediate displacement risk.
callused fingers on a model, the bandana at his hip, everything honest and worn.
If I can add—these structures are designed to be modular.
They can be installed in phases to match funding and community input.
They don't lock neighborhoods into displacement.
Exactly.
Phasing means work crews from the neighborhood are employed first; monitoring is community-led.
We scale with people, not just capital.
And the monitoring plan includes independent verification and a city-held escrow for environmental outcomes.
We can report quarterly to the council.
Councilor Anika leans forward, eyes sharpening.
'That's the implementation piece I'm interested in.
Funding timelines, oversight—how do you ensure this isn't undercut by private contractors promising faster, cleaner results?'
You feel the room tilt toward that question as if the air itself is heavier where NovaSeis's name lingers.
Elias Crowe sits across the room, an immaculate presence with a set of polished models on a raised platform beside him.
His smile is a cut of economy—measured, efficient.
With respect, Councilor, when the stakes are metropolitan safety, speed matters.
Our solutions are engineered to guarantee levels of protection within regulatory targets.
You won't need to wait for years of incremental improvement.
Speed without ecological compatibility brings externalities.
Seawalls can protect downtown but accelerate erosion elsewhere.
We can't trade one community's safety for another's loss.
Elias Crowe's expression flickers—brief, like sunlight off steel.
He answers smoothly, untroubled.
Our models account for redistributed stress.
We propose compensatory measures.
The city gets certainty.
Compensatory measures are often distant from the affected community.
The ecological services that marshes and berms provide are not fungible—sediment capture, nursery habitat—those are local.
Councilor Anika narrows her attention between them, the scales of political pressure visibly adjusting in her posture.
I need assurances on both risk and optics.
If we move too slowly, voters will hold us responsible.
If we move too fast, we risk legal and ethical fallout.
A woman from the back—Maya's voice, or someone like her—stands and speaks about the creak of her floors when the tides rise, about children learning to fish in new marsh channels.
Her testimony lands like a pebble making rings.
You feel your logic and your stubborn heart braid together.
The data isn't abstract here; it's living rooms and lunches and the smell of brine on someone's hair.
Jonah Reyes catches your hand under the table—a quick, grounding press.
He doesn't need to speak for you to feel anchored.
Make it human.
Start with who loses if we don't.
We should make the case with both charts and stories.
The council needs facts they can defend and a narrative the public trusts.
And a monitoring plan the city can sign onto—transparency matters.
If we can show early wins, the political will will follow.
open with a resident testimony

show the model projections immediately
erosion lines recede in staged visuals, and the chamber's attention tightens on the numbers rather than the noise.

You sharpen your phrasing and cite case studies where quick engineering displaced communities.
A few councilors frown; a handful of activists nod vigorously.
You wake inside the decision you already made.
It has a weight to it—less like a stone and more like a tide that will reshape everything it touches.

printing, cross-checking, asking uncomfortable questions until the seams show.
fans whisper, a spectrometer clicks, and the projector throws slow-moving sediment models across a bulkhead.
We have a timeline.

If we sequence this right, it reads as pattern, not paranoia.
Pattern, not paranoia,' you repeat, tasting the phrase as if it were a knot you can untie.
'We need to show not only the numbers but the people those numbers erase.
Professor Asha looks at you the way she looks at a problem set—calm, exacting, and quietly proud.
Then we start with coastal transects and with testimony.
The dossier should marry the geology and the memory.
Maya moves between a printer and a rack of leaflets, fingers stained with ink and tide-mud.
She hums under her breath—an arranging of energy more like percussion than song.
People will show if they think someone finally understands what gets lost when 'efficiency' is the only measure.
They need to hear their story reflected back in hard paper.
Jonah Reyes stands near the rail, jacket zipped, camera strap across his chest like a talisman.
He hasn't said much.
His municipal connections are a bargaining chip you didn't know you would have to spend; he watches every face in the lab with an engineer's economy of movement.
I can give you shots from the southern breakwater—before and after NovaSeis installed their panels.
Field notes, timestamps.
But if this goes public, it draws lines that might make my office...
uncomfortable.
You turn to him.
Rain throws silver streaks across his hair; his eyes are patient and guarded.
We don't need sacrifice theatrics, Jonah.
We need truth.
If your notes make the case tighter, they save us from being dismissed as sentimental.
Jonah Reyes's jaw tightens.
He rubs his thumb over the woven ring on his right thumb.
I want to help.
But know I won't be a prop.
If this starts a fire, I'll stand with the community, not with any department that tries to quiet it.
Then stand.
Help us light what needs to be seen.
The exchange is not neat; it never is.
Jonah Reyes shifts from guarded to wry in a breath.
Practical lighting then.
No torches.
Just evidence with good exposure.

'This is about what happens when living things are treated like variables.' The lab smells like ink and resolve.
• Lead with the quantitative anomalies
You stack the graphs in front of Asha and lay your pen along a jagged line.
Numbers sharpen conversation.
The room listens differently when the spikes are framed as risk, not rumor.
The group continues assembling materials; evidence and testimony begin to take shape.
Professor Asha pulls up an overlay on the tablet—sites in two other cities where NovaSeis systems were installed.
The overlay blurs; a cursor highlights a radius where shoreline retreat accelerated after intervention.
See here.
Short-term gain, long-term export of sediment.
Proprietary designs that don't account for littoral transport.
If we can pair this with local erosion markers and persistent testimonials—
—then the narrative can't be gaslighted away by glossy ads.
You stack a set of printed comparisons, the pages slightly curled from the humidity.
Jonah Reyes feeds you a flash drive, the metal cool in your palm.
These are raw.
No filters.
